public List<String> removeSubfolders(String[] folder) {

   Arrays.sort(folder);

   List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();

   result.add(folder[0]);

   for (int i=1; i < folder.length; i++) {

       String current = folder[i];

       String last = result.get(result.size()-1);

       // check if current is subfolder of last

       if (!current.startsWith(last + "/")) {

           result.add(current);

       }

   }

   return result;

}

Now, what about the edge case where a folder has a parent later in the sorted array? No, because the sorted array ensures that parents come before children. For example, if there's a folder "/a/b" and "/a", then during sorting, "/a" comes before "/a/b".

class Solution {
    public List<String> removeSubfolders(String[] folder) {
        Arrays.sort(folder);
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
        if (folder.length == 0) return result; // but constraints say folder.length >=1
        result.add(folder[0]);
        for (int i = 1; i < folder.length; i++ ) {
            String current = folder[i];
            String last = result.get(result.size()-1);
            if (!current.startsWith(last + "/")) {
                result.add(current);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}

Wait, but what about when the folder array is empty? But according to constraints, folder.length is at least 1. So no need to handle that.

So the code is as above.

Now, let's check for some edge cases.

Edge case 1: folder = ["/a"], output is ["/a"].

Edge case 2: folder = ["/a", "/b/c", "/b"]. After sorting, it's ["/a", "/b", "/b/c"]. The code adds "/a". Then checks "/b" against "/a/": not starting, adds. Then checks "/b/c" against "/b": starts with, so skips. Output is ["/a", "/b"].

Which is correct.

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity: O(n log n) for the sorting step. Then O(n) for the iteration. So overall O(n log n).

Space: O(1) if we ignore the output, but since the output can be up to O(n), but the problem requires returning it. But for the purpose of this problem's answer, perhaps the space is O(n) because the output can be up to n elements. But the code uses a list that grows up to O(n) in the worst case (when there are no subfolders). So the space complexity is O(n) for the output. But in terms of the algorithm's auxiliary space, it's O(1) (if we consider the output not part of the space). But perhaps the problem counts the output as part of space.

But for the problem's efficiency, the time is O(n log n), space O(1) (excluding output). So the efficiency is high.

The energy implications? Well, since the algorithm is efficient, it's low.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public List<String> removeSubfolders(String[] folder) {
        Arrays.sort(folder);
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
        result.add(folder[0]);
        for (int i = 1; i < folder.length; i++) {
            String current = folder[i];
            String last = result.get(result.size() - 1);
            if (!current.startsWith(last + "/")) {
                result.add(current);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n log n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution sorts the folders lexicographically, ensuring parent folders precede subfolders. It then iterates once, checking if each folder is a subfolder of the last added parent by string prefix comparison. This avoids redundant checks by leveraging sorted order. Key strength is O(n log n) efficiency, weakness is reliance on lexicographical ordering which may not generalize to all path conventions.
